insights.title: Scripting access to convolution node via external audio file slot
insights.summary: The filters.convolution node (which wraps the stock Convolution Reverb) cannot be accessed directly from script — this would prevent later C++ compilation of the network. Instead, set the audio file slot to 'External' in the node UI, then use `Synth.getAudioSampleProcessor("ScriptFX")` to get the parent ScriptFX reference and call `getAudioFile(0)` on it. This pattern works identically when the network is compiled to a hardcoded C++ FX module.
insights.verify_hint: Confirm that the external slot button exists in the filters.convolution node UI and that the getAudioFile(index) call propagates correctly to the compiled C++ node.

insights.title: IRs must be loaded into pool before export or setFile() calls
insights.summary: Calling `AudioSampleProcessor.setFile()` with embedded IR files requires that `Engine.loadAudioFilesIntoPool()` is called first. Without this, embedded audio resources are missing in the exported plugin. HISE now throws a script error to catch this case. Do not use `Engine.findFiles()` — IR files do not exist on the user's system; use references returned from `loadAudioFilesIntoPool()` instead.

insights.title: Correct scripting path to load an IR into a scriptnode convolution reverb
insights.summary: The pattern is: (1) use a `filters.convolution` node, not `core.file_player`; (2) get the audio processor reference via `Synth.getAudioSampleProcessor("ScriptFXName")`; (3) call `setFile(relativePath)` on it. There is no need to call `getAudioFile(1)` as an intermediate step.
insights.verify_hint: Verify that Synth.getAudioSampleProcessor on a ScriptFX node name returns a valid reference and setFile accepts the relative AudioFiles path.

insights.title: Reverb always spreads a hard-panned signal across the full stereo field
insights.summary: The fx.reverb (and the underlying algorithm) always creates a stereo output from a mono or panned input — this is expected diffusion behaviour, not a bug. A hard-panned signal will produce reverb that is spread across the entire stereo field. Confirmed by the author as intentional. Use a goniometer on channels 3+4 to verify the stereo field if in doubt.

insights.title: Clicks on IR change in convolution reverb acknowledged but unresolved
insights.summary: Switching impulse responses during playback produces audible clicks. The author acknowledged this was addressed at some point but regressed. Flagged as a known TODO item as of 2020. No built-in crossfade or gapless switch exists; users should plan IR changes during silence if clicks are a problem.
insights.verify_hint: Check if this has been resolved in recent HISE builds by switching IRs during playback.

insights.title: Reverb room size has no simple mapping to decay time in seconds
insights.summary: The Room Size parameter does not have a linear or simple transfer function to decay time in seconds — the actual decay also depends on width and damping. The author's recommended approaches are: (1) run an impulse through the reverb, measure the time to -60 dB in a wave editor for several Room Size values, then interpolate; or (2) build a fantasy transfer function that matches perception.

insights.title: getCurrentLevel() on Convolution Reverb does not expose separate wet/dry meters
insights.summary: There is no built-in scripting API to read the wet and dry levels independently from the Convolution Reverb. `getCurrentLevel(0/1)` returns the post-mix output, not the wet signal alone. Source inspection confirms: dryMeter = input signal, wetMeter = output signal. A workaround is to subtract the known dry level from the output reading, or to place a meter module before the reverb.
insights.verify_hint: Check whether a wet-only meter was added to the scripting API for Convolution Reverb after 2020.

insights.title: Using compressor reduction to drive reverb ducking without a true sidechain
insights.summary: Without a proper sidechain implementation in HISE, a reverb duck-on-transient effect can be approximated by: routing the compressor so its audio output goes nowhere, then using the compressor's GainReduction modulation output to drive a `simple_gain` node placed on the reverb's wet path. This was noted by a trusted community member as a workable pattern.
insights.verify_hint: Verify that the compressor modulation output can target a gain on a send/wet chain and that the routing produces the expected pumping effect.
